title: Ensure only MFA enabled identities can access privileged  Virtual Machine

input_row.title: Ensure only MFA enabled identities can access privileged  Virtual Machine
input_row.description: Verify identities without MFA that can log in to a privileged virtual machine using separate login credentials. An adversary can leverage the access to move laterally and perform actions with the virtual machine's managed identity. Make sure the virtual machine only has necessary permissions, and revoke the admin-level permissions according to the least privileges principal
input_row.rationale: Integrating multi-factor authentication (MFA) as part of the organizational policy can greatly reduce the risk of an identity gaining control of valid credentials that may be used for additional tactics such as initial access, lateral movement, and collecting information. MFA can also be used to restrict access to cloud resources and APIs. An Adversary may log into accessible cloud services within a compromised environment using Valid Accounts that are synchronized to move laterally and perform actions with the virtual machine's managed identity. The adversary may then perform management actions or access cloud-hosted resources as the logged-on managed identity.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Log in to the Azure portal. 2. Select the Subscription, then click on Access control (IAM). 3. Click Role : All and click All to display the drop-down menu. 4. Type Virtual Machine Administrator Login and select Virtual Machine Administrator Login. 5. Review the list of identities that have been assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role. 6. Go to Microsoft Entra ID. 7. For Per-user MFA: 1. Under Manage, click Users. 2. Click Per-user MFA. 3. Ensure that none of the identities assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role from step 4 have Status set to disabled. 8. For Conditional Access: 1. Under Manage, click Security. 2. Under Protect, click Conditional Access. 3. Ensure that none of the identities assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role from step 4 are exempt from a Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for all users.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Log in to the Azure portal. 2. This can be remediated by enabling MFA for user, Removing user access or Reducing access of managed identities attached to virtual machines. • Case I : Enable MFA for users having access on virtual machines. 1. Go to Microsoft Entra ID. 2. For Per-user MFA: 1. Under Manage, click Users. 2. Click Per-user MFA. 3. For each user requiring remediation, check the box next to their name. 4. Click Enable MFA. 5. Click Enable. 3. For Conditional Access: 1. Under Manage, click Security. 2. Under Protect, click Conditional Access. 3. Update the Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for all users, removing each user requiring remediation from the Exclude list. • Case II : Removing user access on a virtual machine. 1. Select the Subscription, then click on Access control (IAM). 2. Select Role assignments and search for Virtual Machine Administrator Login or Virtual Machine User Login or any role that provides access to log into virtual machines. 3. Click on Role Name, Select Assignments, and remove identities with no MFA configured. • Case III : Reducing access of managed identities attached to virtual machines. 1. Select the Subscription, then click on Access control (IAM). 2. Select Role Assignments from the top menu and apply filters on Assignment type as Privileged administrator roles and Type as Virtual Machines. 3. Click on Role Name, Select Assignments, and remove identities access make sure this follows the least privileges principal.
input_row.impact: This recommendation requires the Entra ID P2 license to implement. Ensure that identities that are provisioned to a virtual machine utilizes an RBAC/ABAC group and is allocated a role using Azure PIM, and the Role settings require MFA or use another third-party PAM solution for accessing Virtual Machines.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit process outlined in the control's description can be performed using Azure APIs related to Entra ID for querying MFA status and reviewing role assignments for Virtual Machine access.
- The control directly relates to Azure properties concerning identity and access management, which can be queried via Azure SDK and CLI.
- The use of Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Azure Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) settings are standard and exposed through Azure APIs, making this audit automated.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_entra_id_mfa_enabled_for_privileged_vm_access

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Azure SDK for Python to make API calls to the Entra ID and Azure Resource Management services to query necessary settings.
- Azure CLI commands:
  - Use `az role assignment list` to list role assignments for the Virtual Machine.
  - Use `az ad user show` or the equivalent API in the SDK to check the MFA status of users assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role.
  - Check Conditional Access policies using the `az ad conditional-access policy list` command.

### Steps for Automation:
1. **Query Role Assignments:**
   - Endpoint: Call `az role assignment list` targeting the Virtual Machine.
   - **Pass/Fail Criteria:** Ensure that no identities assigned have disabled MFA.

2. **Check MFA Status for Users:**
   - Endpoint: Use `az ad user show --id {userId}` for each user with Virtual Machine Administrator Login.
   - **Pass/Fail Criteria:** The MFA status of the user must be enabled.

3. **Validate Conditional Access Policies:**
   - Endpoint: Use `az ad conditional-access policy list`.
   - **Pass/Fail Criteria:** Verify that no users assigned to the admin role are exempt from Conditional Access policies that require MFA.

By implementing the automated solution using the outlined Azure APIs and commands, compliance can be efficiently checked against the CIS benchmark requirements.
